item.description:
A function ff between partially ordered sets is monotone if it preserves order:

a≤b  ⟹  f(a)≤f(b)a \leq b \implies f(a) \leq f(b)

Larger inputs give larger (or equal) outputs. The function never reverses the ordering.

Addition of a positive constant is monotone on numbers: if a≤ba \leq b then a+3≤b+3a + 3 \leq b + 3. Meet with a fixed element is monotone on a lattice: if a≤ba \leq b then a∧c≤b∧ca \wedge c \leq b \wedge c. Negation on the integers is NOT monotone: 2≤52 \leq 5 but −2≥−5-2 \geq -5.

The absorption laws are the two identities that tie meet and join together in a lattice:

a∧(a∨b)=aa∨(a∧b)=aa \wedge (a \vee b) = a \qquad a \vee (a \wedge b) = a

The first says: joining aa with bb gives something at least as large as aa, and meeting that with aa recovers aa. The second says: meeting aa with bb gives something at most as large as aa, and joining that with aa recovers aa.

Absorption is what makes a lattice a lattice rather than just a set with two unrelated operations. Without absorption, meet and join could behave independently. Absorption forces them to interact: each operation partially undoes the other.

A bounded lattice is a lattice with a top element ⊤\top and a bottom element ⊥\bot:

  • ⊥≤a\bot \leq a for all aa (bottom is below everything)
  • a≤⊤a \leq \top for all aa (top is above everything)

Every complete lattice is bounded. Every finite lattice is bounded.

The four-valued bilattice is bounded under both its orderings: truth-bottom is ff, truth-top is tt; knowledge-bottom is ⊥\bot, knowledge-top is ⊤\top.

The twist product construction requires a bounded distributive lattice as input.

A complete lattice is a lattice where every subset has a meet (greatest lower bound) and a join (least upper bound) — not just pairs, but arbitrary subsets including infinite ones.

Every complete lattice is bounded: the join of the empty set is the bottom element ⊥\bot, and the meet of the empty set is the top element ⊤\top.

The Knaster-Tarski theorem: every monotone function on a complete lattice has a least fixpoint and a greatest fixpoint. This is the foundation for closure operations in logic programming and bilattice semantics (Fitting 1991).

A concept lattice is the complete lattice of formal concepts arising from a formal context.

A formal context (Ganter & Wille 1999) is a triple (G,M,I)(G, M, I) where GG is a set of objects, MM is a set of attributes, and I⊆G×MI \subseteq G \times M is an incidence relation (I(g,m)I(g, m) holds when object gg has attribute mm). Derivation operators define a Galois connection:

A′={m∈M:∀g∈A, I(g,m)}B′={g∈G:∀m∈B, I(g,m)}A' = \{m \in M : \forall g \in A,\ I(g, m)\} \qquad B' = \{g \in G : \forall m \in B,\ I(g, m)\}

A′A' gives the attributes shared by all objects in AA. B′B' gives the objects sharing all attributes in BB. A formal concept is a pair (A,B)(A, B) with A′=BA' = B and B′=AB' = A. The set AA is the extent (the objects falling under the concept) and BB is the intent (the attributes defining the concept).

A distributive lattice is a lattice where meet distributes over join:

a∧(b∨c)=(a∧b)∨(a∧c)a \wedge (b \vee c) = (a \wedge b) \vee (a \wedge c)

Equivalently, join distributes over meet (either distributive law implies the other).

Distributive lattices are the setting for Priestley duality: every bounded distributive lattice is dually equivalent to a Priestley space (a compact ordered topological space). This gives every distributive lattice a geometric dual.

Every Boolean algebra is distributive. Every Heyting algebra is distributive. The converse is false in both cases.

A fixpoint of a function ff is a value xx satisfying f(x)=xf(x) = x. The function maps xx to itself.

The Knaster-Tarski theorem: every monotone function on a complete lattice has a least fixpoint (and a greatest fixpoint). The least fixpoint is the meet of all pre-fixpoints (xx with f(x)≤xf(x) \leq x).

In logic programming on bilattices, Fitting (1991) used the Knaster-Tarski theorem to show that inference operators on bilattice-valued assignments reach a least fixpoint — the closure of the program under its inference rules. The closure makes all derivable knowledge explicit.

A Galois connection between two partially ordered sets (A,≤)(A, \leq) and (B,≤)(B, \leq) is a pair of maps f:A→Bf: A \to B and g:B→Ag: B \to A satisfying:

f(a)≥bif and only ifa≥g(b)f(a) \geq b \quad\text{if and only if}\quad a \geq g(b)

(antitone form) or equivalently a pair of monotone maps between opposite orders. This is the order-theoretic form of an adjunction.

The central example is formal concept analysis (Ganter & Wille 1999): given a formal context (G,M,I)(G, M, I), the derivation operators A↦A′A \mapsto A' and B↦B′B \mapsto B' form a Galois connection between subsets of objects and subsets of attributes. The closed elements of this connection are the formal concepts; they form a concept lattice.

Subsumption is an ordering by specificity. aa subsumes bb (written a⊑ba \sqsubseteq b or b≤ab \leq a) if bb is at least as specific as aa — bb has all the information aa has and possibly more.

In typed feature structures (Carpenter 1992), F1⊑F2F_1 \sqsubseteq F_2 iff every feature-value pair in F1F_1 is present and at least as specific in F2F_2. A feature structure with more specified attributes is more specific.

In type systems, a supertype subsumes a subtype. In ontologies, a general concept subsumes a specific one.

A total order is a partial order where every pair of elements is comparable: for any aa and bb, either a≤ba \leq b or b≤ab \leq a.

The integers under ≤\leq are totally ordered. The real numbers under ≤\leq are totally ordered. Any subset of a totally ordered set is totally ordered.

A total order is also called a linear order or a chain. “Chain” is used especially when referring to a totally ordered subset of a partial order.

A binary relation R on a set S is symmetric if the relation goes both ways: for all a,b∈Sa, b \in S, if aRba \mathrel{R} b then bRab \mathrel{R} a. The direction of the pair does not matter. If a is related to b, b is related to a.

Symmetry says: the relation treats both sides equally. Equality is symmetric — if a=ba = b then b=ab = a. “Is a sibling of” is symmetric. “Is less than” is not: 3<53 < 5 does not give 5<35 < 3.

The join of two elements a and b in a partial order is their least upper bound: the smallest element c such that a≤ca \leq c and b≤cb \leq c.

Formally, a∨b=ca \vee b = c requires two conditions: first, c is an upper bound — a≤ca \leq c and b≤cb \leq c. Second, c is the least such — for any d with a≤da \leq d and b≤db \leq d, we have c≤dc \leq d. The join is unique when it exists, by the same antisymmetry argument as for meet.

A lattice is a partial order in which every pair of elements has a meet (greatest lower bound) and a join (least upper bound).

The meet a∧ba \wedge b and join a∨ba \vee b give the lattice two binary operations. These operations satisfy four laws: commutativity (a∧b=b∧aa \wedge b = b \wedge a), associativity (a∧(b∧c)=(a∧b)∧ca \wedge (b \wedge c) = (a \wedge b) \wedge c), idempotence (a∧a=aa \wedge a = a), and absorption (a∧(a∨b)=aa \wedge (a \vee b) = a and a∨(a∧b)=aa \vee (a \wedge b) = a). Absorption is the law that ties meet and join together — it says that combining an element with something larger than it (via join) and then taking the meet recovers the original.

The meet of two elements a and b in a partial order is their greatest lower bound: the largest element c such that c≤ac \leq a and c≤bc \leq b.

Formally, a∧b=ca \wedge b = c requires two conditions: first, c is a lower bound — c≤ac \leq a and c≤bc \leq b. Second, c is the greatest such — for any d with d≤ad \leq a and d≤bd \leq b, we have d≤cd \leq c. The meet is unique when it exists, because if two elements are both greatest lower bounds, antisymmetry forces them to be equal.

A preorder on a set S is a binary relation ≤\leq that is reflexive (a≤aa \leq a for all a) and transitive (a≤ba \leq b and b≤cb \leq c imply a≤ca \leq c).

A preorder is the weakest structure that supports a notion of “at least as much as.” It allows ties: elements a and b can satisfy both a≤ba \leq b and b≤ab \leq a without being equal. When ties are ruled out by adding antisymmetry, the preorder becomes a partial order.

item.title: Antisymmetric
item.description:
A relation R on a set S is antisymmetric if mutual relatedness implies equality: whenever aRb and bRa, then a = b.

Antisymmetry is what distinguishes a partial order from a preorder. A preorder allows “ties” where a ≤ b and b ≤ a without a = b (the elements are equivalent but not identical). A partial order rules this out: the only way for two elements to be mutually related is for them to be the same element.

item.title: Partial Order
item.description:
A partial order on a set is a relation ≤ that satisfies three rules. It is reflexive: everything is ≤ itself. It is transitive: if a ≤ b and b ≤ c, then a ≤ c. It is antisymmetric: if a ≤ b and b ≤ a, then a and b are the same thing. A set with a partial order on it is called a partially ordered set, or poset.

“Partial” means not everything has to be comparable. Two elements can just have no relation to each other — neither one is ≤ the other. This is what separates a partial order from a total order, where every pair must be comparable. Numbers with ≤ are a total order. Sets with ⊆ are a partial order — {1,2} and {2,3} are not comparable because neither contains the other.

item.title: Reflexive
item.description:
A relation R on a set S is reflexive if every element is related to itself: for all a ∈ S, aRa holds.

Reflexivity is one of the three defining properties of a partial order (along with transitivity and antisymmetry) and one of the two defining properties of a preorder (along with transitivity).

Reflexivity says: every element is at least as good as itself, every point is accessible from itself, every object has an identity morphism. In a poset viewed as a category, reflexivity corresponds to the existence of identity morphisms: the morphism a → a witnesses that a ≤ a.

item.title: Transitive
item.description:
A relation R on a set S is transitive if it chains: whenever aRb and bRc, then aRc.

Transitivity is one of the three defining properties of a partial order and one of the two defining properties of a preorder. It captures the idea that the relation is coherent across intermediate steps — if a ≤ b and b ≤ c, then a ≤ c directly, without needing b as a witness.
